channel.title: Comments for page "The Meaning of Fear"
channel.description: Posts in the discussion thread "The Meaning of Fear"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
[EXPLETIVE REMOVED], that actually freaked me out!

Have my +1 you beautiful son of a gun!

item.title: Audio Adaptation
item.content:encoded:
I'm a fan of "You better watch out for the goofy ones."

Audio Adaptation

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Holy crap this is good
+1
Excellent use of mood whiplash, and congrats on +100

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: something tells me that someone may be watching hitchock or something haha

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Hey, I remember this from chat. This turned out beautifully, congrats. I really feel that anyone who reads the scip and not the tale is doing themselves a disservice.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Ok, Now you turned this into a memetic menace. Now the scared researchers have to be terminated just so the other ones can do their job unscared. That researcher could severely decrease the morale of that site.

Who gave that SCP that information?

We need our researchers to be not scared.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+1

Good job. I really enjoyed this one. I'm still not sure if it comprehends what its doing, or if its just trying something new to be scary. Hell, he may not even know if it scared the docter, after all, the SCP-2006 document claims that 2006 is a horrendously bad judge of character.

I think one way I might've done it would be if the Doctor were more professional. He has this cramped pit in his stomach, but he manages to remain composed and just remark after the monologue, "Actually, that doesn't really scare me at all, 2006, I'm pretty sure we have everything under control."

"Oh, really, darn, I thought that might scare you. Hey, BOOOO!!!"

"Waaagh! Ahh, you got me!"

Then the doctor leaves, having maybe/hopefully averted THIS catastrophe, but it certainly seems like 2006 is getting smarter, and was the monologue it for real? Who knows.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I enjoyed the tale, though I think more so because it sounds like a great indicator of bad things to happen. Like a warning to the Foundation about things in motion, well after there is time for them to stop anything from taking place.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+1
+1
+1
I had to re-read that one paragraph (you know, that paragraph) a time or two, but then it hit me.
I literally felt it in my stomach. That was scary. Bravo.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't like this being an alternate version of 2006, because it just highlights how silly the actual 2006 is.

e. And seeing as it IS an alternate version, I'd say there's no reason to be so subtle about the fact that this thing knows how to fuck shit up. Have its form flicker between a nuclear bomb or a world leader or a young pregnant girl strapped to a hospital bed or 106 or whatever.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I suppose that it's a more personal thing, but I'd be a lot more afraid of my former friend being a serial killer. :P

I was more referring to SCP-2006 itself, which is what 2006 was implying by saying it. It could also refer to any infohazardous SCP as well, where simple knowledge of the SCP is enough to cause serious issues.

I kind of wanted to leave the measurements/experiments a little vague. It's not important to the story, and describing them does nothing but detract from the major point that I'm trying to make.

Also, thanks for the heads up. I did a search/replace thing, and forgot that I used smile more than once. Derp.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Or he could have been thinking about ice cream. :) You're assuming he's been a serial killer all this time when he might only have snapped later on in his life. I suppose that opens the door for false hope, though that just takes the fear from you to the audience: "No, you idiot, he'll kill you too!"

Referring to your previous comment, what sort of movie did you have in mind? Analysis of the surveillance footage would pretty much confirm that it's aware of the Foundation and what it does, stuff it couldn't plausibly have picked up from horror movies. I suppose it's remotely possible someone slipped it SCP Foundation: The Movie.

On a tangent, what other SCPs did you have in mind when writing this tale? ("…you have many objects which are contained on the strength of your deception…")

Also, how are they measuring its density/mass/chemical structure-altering abilities? Without betraying that they weren't actually scared of it?

(Also also, I think you may have miscorrected "its smile" to "Its smiled")

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'd be a hell of a lot more scared if one of my friends turned out to be a serial killer. Imagine, all of the times that I hung out with him, he was plotting how to methodically murder people. It's more than a little disturbing.

I meant that the Foundation is going to second-guess itself in the future. In this interpretation, the Foundation would logically begin to question everything it did in the past, and examine what it has done and will do in the future to properly contain this thing.

Finally, remember, it's a shapeshifter, with no known limits. Breaking out of containment is not going to be that hard for it, also considering the fact that it's impossible to damage it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Unless this serial killer had a habit of targeting old buddies, I don't think one would be significantly scarier than the other. Heck, the latter might be less scary, since you'd have some idea of their habits from high school, and how they thought, and know some things to avoid.

The Foundation's second-guessing itself? Where is that in the tale?

Who and how does it plan on scaring next? It's still in containment. Is this scare gonna be big enough for it to live on for the rest of its days?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think that 2006 itself sums it up best: there's fear in a violation of safety, a violation of the norm. It's in it for the biggest scare overall. For example, which would be scarier: finding out that there's a serial killer loose in your neighborhood, or finding out that there's a serial killer loose in your neighborhood who also happened to be your best friend in high school?

It's a similar reason why 076-2 and 682 just aren't scary: they're simply insanely violent, insanely powerful creatures that hate everything. The really scary things are those that make you question your safety. Thus, 2006 would pull off a much bigger scare by lulling people into a false sense of security, and then busting out something like this. If it had started scaring from the start, nobody would ever go in there to check and make sure. Now? Now the Foundation is second guessing itself, and is trying to figure out whether or not this is from a movie, or if there's something genuinely serious about this.

Finally…it's a sapient entity that enjoys scaring the shit out of people. It gets bored with all the theatrics. Once it's convinced that people have been fooled, why wait any longer to whip out the big scare?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I have not said that it requires 2006 to believe such. I will say that it requires a satisfactory reason why it pretends that it believes such. How amused has it been by letting the Foundation think it's a gullible idiot for all this time?

Not canon outside this tale, yes. But you still have the monster that likes to scare things. Wants to be the best at it, even. And the Foundation that doesn't want it to get too scary, that do their best to make it think narminess is the scariest thing, and ensure it has no opportunity to learn otherwise.

In the original, 2006 behaves as though this is working, and up to the middle of this tale, this 2006 behaves identically. In the original, it is obvious why 2006 behaves this way; it genuinely believes Ro-Man is the pinnacle of horror.

In this tale, however, it lays it out for our dear doctor that this is not true, and given how completely its innocent demeanor melted away, and how much knowledge of actual fear it demonstrated, it could never have truly fallen for it. And there lies the problem: why has it been behaving identically to the one that has?

If it wants to be the best at scaring, (and you have stated this as its purpose), and knew all along what humans were really scared of, why has it been putting up a comedy act at all? It could have got its scares at the start. Conversely, if it had a good reason to act like it sucks at scaring, why indicate otherwise? It's still in containment, this is a routine check, nothing about its situation has changed. So why has this reason suddenly become invalid?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Like I said, this is an alternate interpretation. This interpretation does not require 2006 to actually believe in the horror of Ro-Man, but only that it does so out of amusement.

And again this is not canon outside of the tale. In the original, the horror movie diet is effective, but this tale was written up for those who disagreed and had their own headcanon.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You know, if the second half of the conversation had been the one presented in the article, I don't think I'd have had a problem believing the object class.

That said, I appreciate all the mundanities, as pointed out elsewhere, along with the alternate interpretation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Scaring people may be one of its purposes, yes. But there is another, implicit, purpose; the one served by letting the Foundation think it regards Ro-Man as the pinnacle of horror. It wouldn't be doing that if scaring people was its primary goal, would it? Rather counterproductive, in fact.

And that implicit purpose is damaged by making it so blatantly obvious that it's not what they thought it was, that the crappy horror movie diet was not an effective preventative measure. This is why I'm suggesting you be more subtle.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Like I said in the first post, this is an alternate interpretation on SCP-2006. Regardless, it needed visitors in the original post, because it was to keep it focused on the idea of crappy horror movies being scary.

And you have to keep in mind its purpose: Scaring people. I'd say that it pretty damn well succeeded in that, don't you think?

As for the second portion, feel free to write up a tale where that happens, man! I'd love to see a tale where that happens.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
What does it get out of revealing that its containment procedures don't work and probably never have? What does it stand to lose if it does? Cause you know that if that doctor has anything to say about it, it ain't getting visitors anytime soon. Really, if it's been carrying out this deception so that it will look fairly innocent and naive, what's the point of suddenly revealing that it isn't?

It ought to be ambiguous whether it stumbled on such a truly frightening thing by accident, or is secretly messing with the doctor. It definitely shouldn't suddenly start using big words, or changing its happy-go-lucky tone. Perhaps you could see what bits of that message it could piece together from the monster movies it's allowed to see. Something along the lines of "And then the monster finds out it's being tricked… And it pretends it doesn't know until someone next visits it… and then… BOO! … HAHA, GOTCHA" And then the reaction: Oh, phew, it hasn't figured things out. And after he leaves: I think…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Good SCP, good Tale. Great job, weizhong. Keep up the great work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: And this is why it's SCP-2006 and not SCP-2006-J.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is just incredible, just take all of my 1+s.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Part of the original point of SCP-2006 is that the Foundation has to check up on it, just to ensure that it's still "contained" in that it still believes that "Craptastic Horror Movie A" is the scariest thing in the world. Bad Move? Yes. Necessary Move? Also yes.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I find the horror tempered by the fact that the guy is in the cell in the first place, talking with it. Bad Move Number 1. On top of that, he's basically saying "you aren't as safe as you think", which is pretty obvious considering they've already made Bad Move Number 1 and probably do it elsewhere, with other objects.

But well-written nonetheless.

item.title: Oh My...
item.content:encoded: No words can describe the face that I made when I Finished this tale. It genuinely scared me. This, my friends, is what SCP was founded on. Which is scary stories. Good Job +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I feel like there could be more…but this is unsettling. It's been awhile since something on the site has really unsettled me (I need to change that), but this is good. +1

And I really liked the mundanities of actually entering a containment cell. I found it really interesting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The slug monster, originally seeming so harmless, slowly swiveled its head around to look at Wensley, and smile.

needs the D. Ya know, past tense.

Also, i'm having trouble wrapping my head around "betentacled slug monster" and "smile." Not sure if that's intentional or not.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Dear God….
It's figuring it out..

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Love it. I love seeing things involving different takes on SCP.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Mother of God. That was actually scary. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Interesting alternate character interpretation. Upvoted.

item.title: Author Overview
item.content:encoded:
Two posted things in two days? Weizhong you crazy sonofa

This is a follow-up tale on SCP-2006. A lot of people told me (while I was still drafting 2006) that I should write a companion tale as well. Then, after I actually posted it, a lot of people had complaints, such as "It's too stupid to be Keter," "It's not actually that scary," etc.

So, I wrote up a little tale to be an alternate interpretation on SCP-2006. Up to you if it's the correct one.

Thanks to CryogenChaos, Randomini, Roget, Berggren, EnconXV, CastleBravo, Poke, and others in chat for taking a look at it.

EDIT: One of these days, Kalinin….I'll finally write something good enough for you to upvote. :P
